A cylinder of height $L$ contains a fixed mass of ideal gas. A piston is used to compress the gas isothermally (at constant temperature $T_0$). The initial volume and pressure of the gas are $V_0$ and $P_0$ respectively. After compression, the piston is located at height $\frac{3}{4}L$, so the gas occupies only $\frac{3}{4}$ of its original volume.

Assume the cross-sectional area of the cylinder remains constant throughout the process.

parts.content: State the ideal gas law.
parts.markscheme: $PV = nRT$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the law
steps.content: To answer this, we need to recall the fundamental relationship between the pressure, volume, and temperature of an ideal gas. This concept is a core part of **Topic B.3: Gas laws**.

steps.title: State the molar form
steps.content:
In the IB Physics data booklet, under the **Thermal Physics** section, the ideal gas law is expressed as:

$$PV = nRT$$

In this molar form:
- $P$ is the pressure of the gas.
- $V$ is the volume of the gas.
- $n$ is the number of moles.
- $R$ is the molar gas constant ($8.31 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$).
- $T$ is the absolute temperature (measured in Kelvin).

steps.title: Identify the relevant topic
steps.content: Hi! To answer this, we need to look at **Topic B.3: Gas laws**. The ideal gas law is the equation of state for an ideal gas, relating its pressure, volume, temperature, and the amount of substance present.

steps.title: Locate the formula
steps.content:
In your IB Physics Data Booklet, under the **Thermal Physics** section, you will find the relationship for an ideal gas. 

While the molar form ($PV = nRT$) is very common, we are specifically asked for the molecular form, which uses the total number of molecules ($N$) and the Boltzmann constant ($k_B$).

steps.title: State the molecular equation
steps.content:
Using the molecular form requested, the ideal gas law is stated as:

$$PV = Nk_B T$$

Where:
- $P$ is the pressure of the gas
- $V$ is the volume of the gas
- $N$ is the total number of molecules
- $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant ($1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$)
- $T$ is the absolute temperature (measured in Kelvin)

parts.content: Outline two assumptions of the ideal gas model.
parts.markscheme:
Any two of the following:
- Gas molecules occupy negligible volume compared to the volume of the container <marks>A1</marks>
- There are no intermolecular forces between molecules (except during collisions) <marks>A1</marks>
- All collisions (between molecules and with the walls) are perfectly elastic <marks>A1</marks>
- The duration of collisions is negligible compared to the time between collisions <marks>A1</marks>
- Molecules are in continuous random motion <marks>A1</marks>

<marks>A1A1</marks>

parts.content: State the ratio $\dfrac{V_1}{V_0}$ of the volumes of the gas before ($V_0$) and after ($V_1$) compression.
parts.markscheme:
$V_1 = \dfrac{3}{4} V_0$

$\Rightarrow \dfrac{V_1}{V_0} = \dfrac{3}{4}$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: The temperature of the gas remains constant at $T_0$. Using the ideal gas law, determine the ratio $\dfrac{P_1}{P_0}$ where $P_1$ is the pressure after compression.
parts.markscheme:
For an isothermal process, $PV = \text{constant}$, so $P_0 V_0 = P_1 V_1$ <marks>M1</marks>

$\dfrac{P_1}{P_0} = \dfrac{V_0}{V_1} = \dfrac{1}{\frac{3}{4}} = \dfrac{4}{3}$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Explain why, during this isothermal compression, work is done on the gas.
parts.markscheme:
- The volume of the gas is reduced ($\Delta V < 0$) <marks>A1</marks>
- Work is done when a force (exerted by the piston) acts through a distance in the direction of the force <marks>R1</marks>

parts.content: The gas is then heated at constant volume until its pressure reaches $2P_0$. Determine the final temperature $T_2$ in terms of the initial temperature $T_0$.
parts.markscheme:
At constant volume, $\dfrac{P}{T} = \text{constant}$, so $\dfrac{P_2}{T_2} = \dfrac{P_1}{T_1}$ <marks>M1</marks>

From the isothermal process, $T_1 = T_0$ and $P_1 = \dfrac{4}{3}P_0$

Substituting the values:

$\dfrac{2P_0}{T_2} = \dfrac{\frac{4}{3}P_0}{T_0}$

$T_2 = \dfrac{2}{\frac{4}{3}} T_0 = \dfrac{6}{4} T_0 = \dfrac{3}{2} T_0$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: State the gas law that describes this behaviour.
parts.markscheme: Charles's Law <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the physical conditions
steps.content:
From the question, we identify that the gas is undergoing a change where the **pressure remains constant** while the temperature and volume change. In physics, this is called an isobaric process.

This behavior is a fundamental part of **Topic B.3 Gas laws**, which describes how the macroscopic properties of a gas (pressure, volume, and temperature) relate to one another.

steps.title: Identify the gas law
steps.content:
According to the ideal gas law found in the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:

$$PV=nRT$$

If the pressure ($P$) and the amount of gas ($n$) are held constant, then the volume ($V$) is directly proportional to the absolute temperature ($T$):

$$V \propto T$$

By direct recall of standard gas law definitions, the specific law that describes this proportional relationship between volume and temperature at constant pressure is **Charles's Law**.

steps.title: Start with Ideal Gas Equation
steps.content:
To understand the relationship between volume and temperature, we start with the **Ideal Gas Equation** found in the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

This equation relates the pressure ($P$), volume ($V$), number of moles ($n$), and absolute temperature ($T$) of an ideal gas, where $R$ is the gas constant.

This topic is covered in **Topic B.3: Gas laws**.

steps.title: Identify the constant variables
steps.content:
Next, we look at the conditions given in the question. We are told the gas is "trapped" (meaning the number of moles $n$ is constant) and the gas is heated while the "pressure remains constant". 

Since $n$, $R$, and $P$ are all constant in this scenario, we can rearrange the equation to isolate the relationship between volume ($V$) and temperature ($T$).

steps.title: Deduce the relationship
steps.content:
Rearranging the ideal gas equation to solve for $V$:

$$\begin{aligned} V &= \frac{nRT}{P} \\ V &= \left( \frac{nR}{P} \right) T \end{aligned}$$

Since the term in the parentheses $\left( \frac{nR}{P} \right)$ is a constant, we can see that volume is directly proportional to the absolute temperature:

$$V \propto T$$

steps.title: State the gas law
steps.content:
The specific gas law that states the volume of a fixed mass of gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature at constant pressure is **Charles's Law**.

In an exam, simply stating the name is enough to earn the mark.

parts.content: If the initial volume is $0.050\ \text{m}^3$ at $300\ \text{K}$, calculate the new volume when the temperature is increased to $360\ \text{K}$.
parts.markscheme:
$\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2} \Rightarrow \frac{0.050}{300} = \frac{V_2}{360}$ <marks>M1</marks>
$V_2 = \frac{0.050 \cdot 360}{300} = 0.060\ \text{m}^3$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
From the question, we are given the initial state and the final state of the gas. Since the gas is heated at a constant pressure, we can use the direct ratio between volume and temperature. Let's list what we know:

- Initial volume ($V_1$) = $0.050\ \text{m}^3$
- Initial temperature ($T_1$) = $300\ \text{K}$
- Final temperature ($T_2$) = $360\ \text{K}$
- Final volume ($V_2$) = ?

steps.title: Apply Charles's Law
steps.content:
Because the pressure remains constant, we apply Charles's Law. This law states that for a fixed mass of gas at constant pressure, the volume is directly proportional to its absolute temperature ($V \propto T$). 

This is derived from the Ideal Gas Law found in your data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

Since $P$, $n$, and $R$ are constant, we can rewrite this as the ratio:

$$\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into the ratio formula to solve for the final volume $V_2$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{0.050}{300} &= \frac{V_2}{360} \\ V_2 &= \frac{0.050 \times 360}{300} \end{aligned}$$

Setting up this equation correctly earns you the first mark (**M1**).

ti84.instructions: Enter (0.050 * 360) / 300 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type (0.050 * 360) / 300 in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the new volume using the Charles's Law ratio.

steps.title: State the final volume
steps.content:
Calculating the expression gives:

$$V_2 = 0.060\ \text{m}^3$$

This final answer with units provides the second mark (**A1**). Notice that the temperature increased by $20\%$, so the volume also increased by $20\%$ ($0.050 \times 1.2 = 0.060$), which makes sense!

steps.title: Identify the proportionality
steps.content:
In **Topic B.3 Gas laws**, we learn that for a fixed amount of gas at constant pressure, the volume $V$ is directly proportional to its absolute temperature $T$ (Charles's Law). 

This relationship is derived from the ideal gas equation in the data booklet:

$$PV=nRT$$

Since $P$, $n$, and $R$ are constant, if the temperature increases by a certain factor, the volume must increase by that same scaling factor.

steps.title: Find the scaling factor
steps.content:
First, we determine the scale factor of the temperature increase. This is the ratio of the final temperature ($T_2$) to the initial temperature ($T_1$):

$$\text{Scale Factor} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{360\text{ K}}{300\text{ K}}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 360 divided by 300.

desmos.instructions: Divide 360 by 300.

calculator.description: Calculate the temperature scaling factor.

steps.title: Calculate the final volume
steps.content:
Now, we multiply the initial volume ($V_1$) by this scale factor to find the new volume ($V_2$):

$$\begin{aligned} V_2 &= V_1 \times \left( \frac{T_2}{T_1} \right) \\ V_2 &= 0.050 \times 1.2 \\ V_2 &= 0.060\text{ m}^3 \end{aligned}$$

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for the substitution into the gas law relationship and one mark for the final answer with correct units.

ti84.instructions: Multiply 0.050 by 1.2.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate 0.050 * 1.2.

calculator.description: Multiply the initial volume by the scaling factor.

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
Since the gas is heated at constant pressure, we can use the ideal gas law from the data booklet to understand the relationship between volume ($V$) and absolute temperature ($T$).

$$PV = nRT$$

Because pressure ($P$), the number of moles ($n$), and the gas constant ($R$) are all constant, the volume is directly proportional to the absolute temperature ($V \propto T$). This is known as **Charles's Law**, which you can find in **Topic B.3: Gas Laws**.

steps.title: Calculate temperature increase factor
steps.content:
Using the **Fractional Increase Method**, we first determine the factor by which the absolute temperature has increased. We divide the final temperature by the initial temperature:

$$\text{Multiplier} = \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{360\text{ K}}{300\text{ K}}$$

$$\text{Multiplier} = 1.2$$

desmos.instructions: Type 360 / 300 in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the temperatures.

steps.title: Apply factor to volume
steps.content:
Because volume is directly proportional to temperature, the volume must increase by the same factor (1.2). We apply this to the initial volume:

$$V_2 = V_1 \times 1.2$$
$$V_2 = 0.050\text{ m}^3 \times 1.2$$
$$V_2 = 0.060\text{ m}^3$$

In the IB markscheme, this logic earns the method mark (**M1**) for the setup and the accuracy mark (**A1**) for the final result.

parts.content: Explain the relationship between temperature and volume in this context.
parts.markscheme: At constant pressure, increasing temperature increases particle speed, causing them to occupy a larger volume. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the relevant law
steps.content:
To understand the relationship between temperature ($T$) and volume ($V$), we look at the **ideal gas law** provided in the data booklet. This law relates pressure ($P$), volume, temperature, and the amount of gas ($n$).

This concept is part of **Topic B.3 Gas laws** in your syllabus.

steps.title: Identify constant variables
steps.content: In this scenario, we are told the pressure remains constant. Additionally, because the gas is "trapped" in the cylinder, the number of moles ($n$) remains constant, and $R$ is the universal gas constant.

steps.title: Determine the proportionality
steps.content:
We can rearrange the ideal gas law to isolate volume:

$$\begin{aligned} PV &= nRT \\ V &= \left(\frac{nR}{P}\right)T \end{aligned}$$

Since $n$, $R$, and $P$ are all constant, we see that volume is **directly proportional** to the absolute temperature:

$$V \propto T$$

steps.title: Explain the physical mechanism
steps.content:
To earn the mark based on the markscheme, we must explain *why* this happens microscopically:

1. Increasing the temperature increases the average kinetic energy and **speed** of the gas particles.
2. To maintain a **constant pressure** (the same force per unit area from collisions), the particles must travel further between hits, meaning they must **occupy a larger volume**.

steps.title: Link temperature to kinetic energy
steps.content:
To understand this from a microscopic perspective, we first look at what temperature actually represents for a gas. According to the Kinetic Molecular Theory, the absolute temperature $T$ of an ideal gas is directly proportional to the average random kinetic energy of its particles.

From the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:
$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

This means that as the gas is heated and $T$ increases, the particles gain more kinetic energy and move at higher average speeds.

steps.title: Analyze particle collisions
steps.content:
Pressure is caused by gas particles colliding with the walls of the container. Because the particles are now moving faster (higher $v$), two things happen:
1. They collide with the walls more frequently.
2. Each collision involves a greater change in momentum ($Δp$), which exerts a larger force on the wall.

If the volume were to stay the same, these faster, harder collisions would cause the pressure to rise.

steps.title: Explain the volume expansion
steps.content:
The question specifies that the **pressure remains constant**. For the pressure to stay the same while particles are moving faster, the frequency of collisions must decrease to compensate for the harder hits.

By increasing the volume, the particles have to travel further between the walls, and the total surface area of the container increases. This maintains a constant rate of momentum transfer to the walls, keeping the pressure steady. 

Therefore, at constant pressure, an increase in temperature results in an increase in volume (Charles's Law).

questions.specification:
Two rigid containers are connected by a closed valve.

- The left container has volume $9\ \mathrm{dm^3}$ and contains gas at pressure $12\ \mathrm{atm}$ and temperature $T$.
- The right container has volume $6\ \mathrm{dm^3}$ and contains gas at pressure $8\ \mathrm{atm}$ and the same temperature $T$.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/Y6Jj0UKWxWLwlyHhYqOfj.jpeg)

The valve is then opened and the gases are allowed to mix and reach equilibrium. Assume temperature remains constant and the gases are ideal and non-reactive.

steps.title: State the equation

To earn the mark, you simply need to state the equation as it appears in the data booklet. The ideal gas law is expressed as:

$$pV = nRT$$

Alternatively, using the number of molecules ($N$) and the Boltzmann constant ($k_B$), it can be stated as:

steps.title: Recall the Ideal Gas Law
steps.content:
To find the number of moles ($n$) in each container, we use the ideal gas law found in the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

Since we need to express the number of moles in terms of $R$ and $T$, we rearrange the equation to solve for $n$:

$$n = \frac{PV}{RT}$$

$$n_1 = \frac{12 \times 9}{RT}$$

Calculating the numerator:

$$n_1 = \frac{108}{RT}$$

steps.title: Identify the Ideal Gas Law
steps.content:
To solve this using the conservation of amount of substance, we first look at the ideal gas law from the data booklet to find a way to express the number of moles ($n$) in each container.

$$PV = nRT \implies n = \frac{PV}{RT}$$

steps.title: Sum the Total Moles
steps.content:
The total number of moles ($n_{\text{total}}$) in the system is the sum of the moles in the left container ($n_L$) and the right container ($n_R$). Since $R$ and $T$ are constant, we can write:

$$\begin{aligned} n_{\text{total}} &= n_L + n_R \\ n_{\text{total}} &= \frac{P_L V_L}{RT} + \frac{P_R V_R}{RT} \\ n_{\text{total}} &= \frac{(12)(9)}{RT} + \frac{(8)(6)}{RT} \\ n_{\text{total}} &= \frac{108 + 48}{RT} = \frac{156}{RT} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine the Total Volume
steps.content:
When the valve is opened, the gas occupies the combined volume of both rigid containers.

$$V_{\text{total}} = 9\ \text{dm}^3 + 6\ \text{dm}^3 = 15\ \text{dm}^3$$

steps.title: Calculate Final Pressure
steps.content:
Now, we apply the ideal gas law to the combined system to find the final pressure ($P_{\text{final}}$). Because the temperature $T$ remains constant, the $RT$ terms will cancel out:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{final}} &= \frac{n_{\text{total}}RT}{V_{\text{total}}} \\ P_{\text{final}} &= \frac{\left(\frac{156}{RT}\right)RT}{15} \\ P_{\text{final}} &= \frac{156}{15} = 10.4\ \text{atm} \end{aligned}$$

This approach earns marks for correctly determining the total volume and performing the final calculation.

steps.title: Determine the total volume

$$V_{\text{total}} = 9\ \mathrm{dm^3} + 6\ \mathrm{dm^3} = 15\ \mathrm{dm^3}$$

steps.title: Calculate left gas partial pressure
steps.content:
Using Dalton's Law approach, we treat the gas from the left container as if it expands to fill the entire $15\ \mathrm{dm^3}$ alone. Since temperature $T$ and the amount of gas $n$ are constant, we use Boyle's Law ($PV = \text{constant}$), derived from the ideal gas law:

$$P_L V_L = P'_{L} V_{\text{total}}$$

Substituting the values for the left gas:
$$\begin{aligned} 12 \times 9 &= P'_{L} \times 15 \\ 108 &= 15 P'_{L} \\ P'_{L} &= \frac{108}{15} = 7.2\ \text{atm} \end{aligned}$$

$$P_R V_R = P'_{R} V_{\text{total}}$$

Substituting the values for the right gas:
$$\begin{aligned} 8 \times 6 &= P'_{R} \times 15 \\ 48 &= 15 P'_{R} \\ P'_{R} &= \frac{48}{15} = 3.2\ \text{atm} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Reference the Ideal Gas Law
steps.content:
To understand the relationship between pressure, volume, and temperature, we look at the **Ideal Gas Law** found in your data booklet in **Topic B.3: Gas laws**:

$$PV = nRT$$

In this equation, $P$ is pressure, $V$ is volume, $n$ is the number of moles, $R$ is the gas constant, and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

steps.title: Pressure as a function
steps.content:
If we rearrange the Ideal Gas Law to solve for pressure ($P$), we get:

$$P = \frac{nRT}{V}$$

This shows us that for a fixed amount of gas ($n$) in a fixed volume ($V$), the pressure is **directly proportional** to the absolute temperature. 

$$\text{If } T \text{ increases} \implies P \text{ increases}$$
$$\text{If } T \text{ decreases} \implies P \text{ decreases}$$

steps.title: Impact of changing temperature
steps.content:
In our calculation for the final pressure, we assume that the temperature $T$ is the same at the start and the end. 

If the temperature were to change during mixing, the final pressure would no longer be simply proportional to the ratio of moles to volume ($n/V$). Instead, the final pressure would depend on the unknown final temperature $T_{final}$. Without knowing this temperature, the calculation for pressure cannot be uniquely determined because the $RT$ terms in the equations would not cancel out.

steps.title: Final justification
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks for explaining these two points:
1.  **Dependency**: The final pressure is a function of the final temperature.
2.  **Proportionality**: If $T$ changes, $P$ is no longer proportional to $n/V$ because the $RT$ terms do not cancel in the calculations.

steps.title: Recall conservation of moles
steps.content:
The fundamental principle here is that the total amount of gas (the number of moles, $n$) does not change when the valve is opened. The sum of the moles in both containers initially must equal the total moles at the end.

$$n_1 + n_2 = n_f$$

steps.title: Use the Ideal Gas Law
steps.content:
From the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet, we can relate pressure ($P$), volume ($V$), and temperature ($T$) to the number of moles using the ideal gas equation:

$$PV = nRT$$

Rearranging for $n$, we get:

$$n = \frac{PV}{RT}$$

steps.title: Substitute into conservation equation
steps.content:
By substituting the expression for $n$ into our conservation equation, we get a relationship for the pressures and volumes:

$$\frac{P_1V_1}{RT_1} + \frac{P_2V_2}{RT_2} = \frac{P_fV_f}{RT_f}$$

steps.title: Perform variable cancellation
steps.content:
If we assume the temperature remains constant ($T_1 = T_2 = T_f = T$), then the term $RT$ is identical in every part of the equation. We can multiply the entire equation by $RT$ to cancel these variables out:

$$\begin{aligned} RT \left( \frac{P_1V_1}{RT} + \frac{P_2V_2}{RT} \right) &= RT \left( \frac{P_fV_f}{RT} \right) \\ P_1V_1 + P_2V_2 &= P_fV_f \end{aligned}$$

This shows that the simple addition of $PV$ products is only mathematically valid because the constant temperature terms cancel each other out.

steps.title: Explain temperature dependency
steps.content:
If the temperature were not constant, the $RT$ terms would not cancel. This means that the final pressure $P_f$ would depend on the final temperature $T_f$. 

In such a case, pressure would no longer be simply proportional to $\frac{n}{V}$ because changes in temperature would also contribute to the final pressure value, making the simplified $P_1V_1 + P_2V_2 = P_fV_f$ calculation incorrect.

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
From the question, we are given that the mass and temperature of the gas are constant. This allows us to use Boyle's Law, which relates pressure and volume. Let's list our known values:

*   Initial Pressure ($P_1$): $100\ \text{kPa}$
*   Initial Volume ($V_1$): $4.0\ \text{m}^3$
*   Final Volume ($V_2$): $2.0\ \text{m}^3$
*   Final Pressure ($P_2$): ?

steps.title: Apply Boyle's Law
steps.content:
When temperature and mass are constant, the product of pressure and volume is constant ($PV = \text{constant}$), leading to the Boyle's Law equation:

$$P_1V_1 = P_2V_2$$

Substituting our known values into the equation:

$$100 \times 4.0 = P_2 \times 2.0$$

steps.title: Calculate final pressure
steps.content:
Now, we solve for $P_2$ by rearranging the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 400 &= 2.0 \times P_2 \\ P_2 &= \frac{400}{2.0} \\ P_2 &= 200\ \text{kPa} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns the final mark for the correct value and unit.

steps.title: Identify the gas relationship
steps.content:
In **Topic B.3 (Gas laws)**, we learn that for a fixed mass of gas at a constant temperature, the pressure $P$ is inversely proportional to the volume $V$. This is known as Boyle's Law. Mathematically, we can see this from the ideal gas equation in your data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

Since $n$, $R$, and $T$ are all constant in this scenario, the product $PV$ must remain constant. This means if the volume decreases, the pressure must increase proportionally.

steps.title: Calculate volume change factor
steps.content:
First, let's look at how the volume changes. The volume is reduced from $4.0\ \text{m}^3$ to $2.0\ \text{m}^3$. We can find the scale factor of this change:

$$\text{Scale factor} = \frac{2.0\ \text{m}^3}{4.0\ \text{m}^3} = 0.5$$

So, the volume has been halved (multiplied by $\frac{1}{2}$).

ti84.instructions: Enter 2.0 divided by 4.0 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 2.0 / 4.0 into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the final volume to the initial volume.

highlights.text: volume of the gas is reduced from 4.0 m^3 to 2.0 m^3

steps.title: Apply proportional reasoning
steps.content:
Because pressure and volume are inversely proportional ($P \propto \frac{1}{V}$), whatever happens to the volume, the **opposite** happens to the pressure.

Since the volume was multiplied by $\frac{1}{2}$, the pressure must be multiplied by the reciprocal, which is $2$. In other words, if you halve the volume, you must double the pressure.

steps.title: Calculate final pressure
steps.content:
Now we simply apply this doubling factor to the initial pressure of $100\ \text{kPa}$:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{\text{final}} &= P_{\text{initial}} \times 2 \\ P_{\text{final}} &= 100\ \text{kPa} \times 2 \\ P_{\text{final}} &= 200\ \text{kPa} \end{aligned}$$

In an IB exam, you earn **1 mark** for showing the correct relationship or substitution and **1 mark** for the final answer including the correct unit ($200\ \text{kPa}$).

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the number of moles ($n$), we use the **Ideal Gas Equation** found in **Topic B.3: Gas laws**. This formula relates pressure, volume, temperature, and the number of moles of a gas.

$$PV = nRT$$

Where:
- $P$ is pressure (in Pa)
- $V$ is volume (in $\text{m}^3$)
- $n$ is the number of moles
- $R$ is the gas constant ($8.31 \text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$)
- $T$ is temperature (in K)

steps.title: Convert units to SI
steps.content:
Before substituting values into the equation, we must ensure all units are in standard SI form. The volume ($0.080\text{ m}^3$) and temperature ($300.0\text{ K}$) are already correct, but the pressure is given in kilopascals (kPa).

Since $1\text{ kPa} = 1000\text{ Pa}$:
$$P = 101 \times 10^3\text{ Pa} = 101,000\text{ Pa}$$

steps.title: Rearrange for moles
steps.content:
To solve for $n$, we rearrange the ideal gas equation:

$$\begin{aligned} PV &= nRT \\ n &= \frac{PV}{RT} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values into our rearranged equation:

$$n = \frac{101,000 \times 0.080}{8.31 \times 300.0}$$

According to the markscheme, the substitution earns the first mark (**M1**). Calculating this gives us approximately $3.24\text{ mol}$ (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To find the root-mean-square (rms) speed using the molar approach, we start with the average kinetic energy of a molecule from the data booklet:

$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

Since average kinetic energy is also defined as $\overline{E_{k}} = \frac{1}{2}m(v_{\text{rms}})^2$, we can relate temperature to speed. By substituting the molar gas constant $R$ and the molar mass $M$, we derive the expression for rms speed:

$$v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{3RT}{M}}$$

- Molar mass ($M$) = $0.028\ \text{kg mol}^{-1}$

$$v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{3 \times 8.31 \times 300.0}{0.028}}$$

Rounding to three significant figures (consistent with the provided data), we get:

$$v_{\text{rms}} = 517 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

$$m = \frac{M}{N_A} = \frac{0.028 \text{ kg mol}^{-1}}{6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}} \approx 4.65 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}$$

steps.content:
Finally, we use the definition of kinetic energy to solve for the root-mean-square speed ($v_{\text{rms}}$):

$$E_{k}=\frac{1}{2}mv^{2} \implies v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{2\overline{E_k}}{m}}$$

Substitute the values we calculated in the previous steps:
$$v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6.21 \times 10^{-21}}{4.65 \times 10^{-26}}} \approx 517 \text{ m s}^{-1}$$

gdc.instructions: Find the square root of (2 * 6.21e-21) divided by (4.65e-26).

desmos.instructions: Use the square root function to find the final speed.

calculator.description: Solve for the rms speed.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The root-mean-square speed of the gas molecules is $517 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. 

This makes sense for a molecule like Nitrogen ($N_2$, molar mass $0.028 \text{ kg mol}^{-1}$) at room temperature! This question relates to **Topic B.3: Gas laws**, which explains how microscopic properties (like speed) lead to macroscopic properties (like temperature).

steps.title: Find the number of moles
steps.content:
To find the total energy and mass, we first need to know the amount of gas in the balloon. We use the ideal gas law from the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

Substituting the given values:
- $P = 101\text{ kPa} = 101,000\text{ Pa}$
- $V = 0.080\text{ m}^3$
- $R = 8.31\text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$ (Gas constant)
- $T = 300.0\text{ K}$

$$\begin{aligned} n &= \frac{PV}{RT} \\ n &= \frac{101,000 \times 0.080}{8.31 \times 300.0} \\ n &\approx 3.241\text{ mol} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate total internal energy
steps.content:
In an ideal monatomic gas, the internal energy is equal to the total kinetic energy of all molecules. The average kinetic energy per molecule is $\frac{3}{2}k_BT$, so for $n$ moles, the total energy ($E_{\text{total}}$) is:

$$E_{\text{total}} = \frac{3}{2}nRT$$

Using our value for $n$:

$$\begin{aligned} E_{\text{total}} &= 1.5 \times 3.241 \times 8.31 \times 300.0 \\ E_{\text{total}} &= 12,120\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate total energy.

steps.title: Calculate total gas mass
steps.content:
Next, we find the total mass ($m_{\text{total}}$) of the gas in the balloon. This is the product of the number of moles and the molar mass ($M$):

$$m_{\text{total}} = n \times M$$

Given $M = 0.028\text{ kg mol}^{-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} m_{\text{total}} &= 3.241 \times 0.028 \\ m_{\text{total}} &\approx 0.09075\text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Calculate total mass.

highlights.text: molar mass of the gas is 0.028 kg mol^-1

steps.title: Solve for rms speed
steps.content:
Finally, we equate the total kinetic energy to the macroscopic kinetic energy formula to find the root-mean-square speed ($v_{\text{rms}}$):

$$E_{\text{total}} = \frac{1}{2}m_{\text{total}}v_{\text{rms}}^2$$

Rearranging for $v_{\text{rms}}$:

$$v_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{2E_{\text{total}}}{m_{\text{total}}}}$$

$$\begin{aligned} v_{\text{rms}} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 12,120}{0.09075}} \\ v_{\text{rms}} &\approx 517\text{ m s}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the relationship
steps.content:
For an **ideal monatomic gas**, the internal energy ($U$) is equal to the total kinetic energy of all its particles. 

From the Data Booklet, the average kinetic energy of a single particle is:
$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

Since total internal energy $U = N\overline{E_{k}}$, and the ideal gas law states $PV = Nk_{B}T$, we can combine these to find the direct relationship:
$$U = \frac{3}{2}PV$$

steps.title: Check and convert units
steps.content:
Before substituting, ensure all values are in SI units. 
- Pressure ($P$): $101\text{ kPa} = 101,000\text{ Pa}$ (or $101 \times 10^3\text{ Pa}$)
- Volume ($V$): $0.080\text{ m}^3$

Notice that the temperature and molar mass given in the question are not required for this specific method, as $PV$ already accounts for those factors.

steps.title: Calculate internal energy
steps.content:
Substitute the pressure and volume into our derived formula:

$$\begin{aligned} U &= \frac{3}{2} \times (101,000\text{ Pa}) \times (0.080\text{ m}^3) \\ U &= 1.5 \times 8080 \\ U &= 12,120\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This calculation earns the first mark (**M1**) for substitution.

$$U = 1.21 \times 10^4\ \text{J}$$

This final value earns the second mark (**A1**).

parts.content: Derive the relationship $P = \frac{1}{3} \rho v^2$ for an ideal gas, where $\rho$ is the density and $v$ is the root mean square (rms) speed of gas particles.
parts.markscheme:
- Consider a particle of mass $m$ in a cubic container of side $L$ moving with velocity component $v_x$.
- The change in momentum per collision with a wall perpendicular to the x-axis is $2mv_x$. The time between collisions is $\frac{2L}{v_x}$. The force exerted is $F_x = \frac{2mv_x}{2L/v_x} = \frac{mv_x^2}{L}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- The pressure is the sum of forces divided by area $L^2$: $P = \frac{N}{L^2} \frac{m \overline{v_x^2}}{L} = \frac{Nm \overline{v_x^2}}{V} = \rho \overline{v_x^2}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Assuming random motion (isotropy), $\overline{v_x^2} = \frac{1}{3} \overline{v^2}$. Thus $P = \frac{1}{3} \rho v^2$. <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Discuss the limitations of this model when applied to real gases, particularly in varying temperature and pressure conditions.
parts.markscheme:
### Limitations of the model

- The ideal gas model assumes that gas particles do not interact with each other and have negligible volume. <marks>A1</marks>
- In real gases, at high pressures and low temperatures, the particle interactions and finite volume become significant, causing deviations from ideal gas behavior. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<commonMistake>
Failing to recognize that the ideal gas model breaks down at high pressures and low temperatures.
</commonMistake>

<examTip>
Mention the assumptions of the ideal gas model and how they are violated in real gases under certain conditions.
</examTip>

steps.title: Identify ideal gas assumptions
steps.content:
To understand the limitations of the ideal gas model, we first need to identify the key assumptions made by the kinetic molecular theory. The model assumes that gas particles behave as point masses (with **negligible volume**) and that there are **no intermolecular forces** acting between them (except during collisions).

This behavior is described by the ideal gas law found in the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet:

$$PV=nRT$$

highlights.text: The ideal gas model assumes that gas particles do not interact with each other and have negligible volume.

steps.title: Analyze high-pressure conditions
steps.content:
When a real gas is under **high pressure**, the particles are forced much closer together. In the ideal model, we assume the particles take up no space. However, in reality, the particles have a finite size.

As the total volume of the gas decreases at high pressure, the volume occupied by the particles themselves becomes a significant fraction of the total volume. This violates the assumption of **negligible volume**.

steps.title: Analyze low-temperature conditions
steps.content:
At **low temperatures**, the average kinetic energy of the particles decreases, as shown by the relationship:

$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

Because the particles are moving more slowly, the small **intermolecular forces** (like van der Waals forces) have more time to act during encounters. This means the particles are no longer independent of each other, violating the assumption that there are no interactions between particles.

steps.title: Synthesize the final answer
steps.content:
To earn both marks, you must state the assumptions and the specific conditions where they fail. 

1. The model assumes particles have **negligible volume** and **no interactions**.
2. Deviations occur at **high pressures** (where volume is significant) and **low temperatures** (where interactions are significant).

highlights.text: In real gases, at high pressures and low temperatures, the particle interactions and finite volume become significant, causing deviations from ideal gas behavior.

steps.title: Define the Ideal Gas Assumptions
steps.content:
To understand the limitations, we first look at the assumptions of the ideal gas model used in the equation from your data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

This model assumes two key things about gas particles:
1. Particles have **negligible volume** (they are treated as point masses).
2. There are **no intermolecular forces** acting between the particles.

In **Topic B.3 Gas Laws**, you learn that while these assumptions work well for many atmospheric conditions, they fail when the physical state of the gas becomes extreme.

steps.title: Analyze High Pressure Conditions
steps.content:
When a real gas is under **high pressure**, the particles are forced much closer together. 

In the ideal model, we assume the volume of the particles themselves is zero. However, at high pressure, the space between particles becomes so small that the **finite volume** of the particles actually takes up a significant portion of the total volume. This means the 'available' space for the gas to move in is less than the model predicts, leading to a deviation from the ideal behavior.

steps.title: Analyze Low Temperature Conditions
steps.content:
At **low temperatures**, gas particles lose kinetic energy and move much more slowly. 

Because they are moving slowly, the **intermolecular forces** (attractions) between them have more time to act. Instead of bouncing off walls perfectly, particles are 'pulled' by their neighbors, which reduces the force of their impact on the container walls. This causes the real pressure to be lower than the ideal pressure predicted by the model.

steps.title: Summary of Model Limitations
steps.content:
To earn the full 2 marks, you must connect these specific state conditions to the violation of the model's assumptions:

- **Mark 1:** State that the model assumes negligible particle volume and no intermolecular interactions.
- **Mark 2:** Explain that at **high pressures** and **low temperatures**, these factors become significant, causing real gases to deviate from the ideal gas law.

steps.title: Ideal Gas Internal Energy
steps.content:
To understand the limitations of the ideal gas model, we first look at its definition of internal energy. In an ideal gas, we assume there are **no intermolecular forces** between particles. Because there are no forces, there is no potential energy stored between them. Therefore, the internal energy of an ideal gas is composed **entirely of random kinetic energy** ($E_k$).

This is supported by the data booklet formula for the average kinetic energy of a molecule, which depends only on temperature:
$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

steps.title: Low Temperature Limitations
steps.content:
As the temperature decreases, the average kinetic energy of the particles also decreases. When the particles move more slowly, the **intermolecular forces** (which are ignored in the ideal model) have more time to act during collisions. 

In a real gas at low temperatures, these attractive forces become significant, meaning the gas now has **potential energy**. Since the ideal gas model assumes potential energy is zero, it fails to accurately predict the gas's behavior as it approaches condensation.

steps.title: High Pressure Limitations
steps.content:
At high pressures, gas particles are forced much closer together. The ideal gas model assumes that the volume of the particles themselves is negligible compared to the volume of the container. 

However, at high pressure, the **finite volume of the particles** becomes a significant fraction of the total volume. Furthermore, because the particles are so close, the intermolecular potential energy becomes a major part of the total internal energy, leading to deviations from the ideal gas law ($PV=nRT$).

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
The ideal gas model is limited because it neglects particle volume and intermolecular forces. These factors become significant at:
1.  **Low temperatures**: Kinetic energy is low enough that intermolecular potential energy cannot be ignored.
2.  **High pressures**: Particles are close enough that their own volume and mutual attractions affect the system.

To earn full marks, ensure you mention both the specific assumptions (no interactions/negligible volume) and the specific conditions (high pressure/low temperature) where they fail.

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
The problem asks for the specific heat capacity $c$ using the relationship between the change in internal energy $\Delta U$ and temperature change $\Delta T$. From **Topic B.1: Thermal energy transfers**, we use the specific heat capacity equation found in the **Thermal Physics** section of the data booklet.

$$Q = mc\Delta T$$

In this context, the energy absorbed by the gas increases its internal energy, so we write the equation as:

$$\Delta U = mc\Delta T$$

This equation is linear ($y = kx$), meaning the ratio $\frac{\Delta U}{\Delta T}$ is constant and represents the term $mc$.

steps.title: Calculate the gradient of the data
steps.content:
Using the **Gradient Method**, we determine the constant ratio $\frac{\Delta U}{\Delta T}$ by calculating the gradient (slope) of the data. We can select two distinct data points, such as Trial 1 and Trial 5, to find the change in $\Delta U$ over the change in $\Delta T$.

$$\text{Gradient} = \frac{\Delta U_5 - \Delta U_1}{\Delta T_5 - \Delta T_1}$$

Substituting the values from the table:

$$\text{Gradient} = \frac{4,500 - 900}{10.0 - 2.0} = \frac{3,600}{8.0} = 450 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

This gradient represents the product of mass and specific heat capacity ($mc$).

ti84.instructions: Calculate the slope (3600/8)

desmos.instructions: Calculate the slope (3600/8)

calculator.description: Calculate the gradient using the difference between Trial 5 and Trial 1

steps.title: Solve for specific heat capacity
steps.content:
Now that we have the gradient $mc = 450 \text{ J K}^{-1}$, we can find the specific heat capacity $c$ by dividing by the mass of the gas given in the question.

$$\begin{aligned} mc &= 450 \\ 0.50 \times c &= 450 \\ c &= \frac{450}{0.50} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculation confirms the specific heat capacity.

$$c = 900 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$$

This matches the value required by the question.

parts.content: Given that the gas is in a sealed, rigid container, calculate the work done on or by the gas during the process in each trial.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the volume does not change in a sealed rigid container (isochoric process). <marks>R1</marks>

- Conclude that work done is zero: $W=0 \mathrm{~J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Define thermodynamic work
steps.content:
To calculate the work done by or on the gas, we apply the standard thermodynamic definition of work. The work $W$ done by a gas depends on the pressure $p$ and the change in volume $\Delta V$:

$$W = p\Delta V$$

steps.title: Identify volume constraint
steps.content: We need to determine the change in volume $\Delta V$. The question states that the experiment is conducted in a specific type of container.

steps.title: Determine change in volume
steps.content:
A **rigid** container has fixed walls that cannot expand or contract. This means the volume of the gas remains constant, regardless of the pressure or temperature changes. Therefore, the change in volume is zero:

$$\Delta V = 0$$

steps.title: Calculate work done
steps.content:
Now we substitute $\Delta V = 0$ into the work equation. Since the volume does not change, the gas does not push against the container walls to move them, meaning no mechanical work is performed.

$$W = p \times 0 = 0 \text{ J}$$

desmos.instructions: Calculate the product:

calculator.description: Verify the calculation for work done

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The work done is $0 \text{ J}$ for every trial. In thermodynamics, a process occurring at constant volume is known as an **isochoric** process.

Following the markscheme, we identify that the volume does not change and conclude the work done is zero.

steps.title: Define mechanical work
steps.content:
To find the work done, we use the mechanical definition of work, which relates force and displacement. From the **Mechanics** section of the data booklet:

$$W = Fs\cos\theta$$

Here, $F$ represents the force exerted by the gas on the container walls, and $s$ represents the displacement of those walls.

steps.title: Analyze displacement
steps.content:
The question specifies that the gas is in a **sealed, rigid container**. In physics, a "rigid" container means its walls cannot expand or contract, so the volume remains constant.

Since the walls do not move, the displacement $s$ is zero.

steps.title: Calculate the work done
steps.content:
Substituting the displacement $s = 0$ into our work equation:

$$W = F \times 0$$

$$W = 0 \text{ J}$$

Since there is no displacement of the container walls, no mechanical work is done by or on the gas in any of the trials.

The work done is **0 J**.

This process is known as an **isochoric** (or isovolumetric) process because the volume remains constant. In such processes, all energy supplied ($Q$) goes directly into changing the internal energy ($	U$) of the gas, as no energy is transferred via work.

parts.content: Using the data from Table 1, calculate the efficiency of the energy transfer for Trial 2, where $E=2.0 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J}$ and $\Delta U=1,800 \mathrm{~J}$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the formula for efficiency: $\eta=\frac{\Delta U}{E} \times 100$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute values for Trial 2: $\eta=\frac{1,800}{2.0 \times 10^3} \times 100=90 \%$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: State the efficiency formula
steps.content:
The efficiency $\eta$ of an energy transfer is defined as the ratio of useful energy output to the total energy input. In this experiment, the useful energy is the change in internal energy $\Delta U$, and the total input is the energy supplied $E$.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{useful energy out}}{\text{total energy in}} \times 100 = \frac{\Delta U}{E} \times 100$$

steps.title: Substitute Trial 2 values
steps.content:
From the question specification for Trial 2, we have the energy supplied $E = 2.0 \times 10^3 \text{ J}$ and the change in internal energy $\Delta U = 1,800 \text{ J}$. Substituting these directly into the formula:

$$\eta = \frac{1,800}{2.0 \times 10^3} \times 100$$

steps.title: Calculate the efficiency
steps.content:
First, convert the scientific notation $2.0 \times 10^3$ to $2,000$ to simplify the division:

$$\eta = \frac{1,800}{2,000} \times 100$$

$$\eta = 0.90 \times 100$$

$$\eta = 90\%$$

parts.content:
The volume of the air is halved slowly while keeping the temperature constant.

Explain, using the kinetic theory of gases, how the pressure changes.
parts.markscheme:
Explain that as volume decreases at constant temperature, the molecules hit the walls more frequently <marks>R1</marks>

State that this results in an increase in pressure <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 2 marks]

steps.title: Analyze molecular speed
steps.content:
In the kinetic theory of gases, the average kinetic energy of the molecules is directly proportional to the absolute temperature of the gas. This relationship is given by the formula:

$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

Since the question states the temperature is kept constant, the average kinetic energy and, consequently, the **average speed** of the molecules remains unchanged.

steps.title: Evaluate collision frequency
steps.content:
When the volume of the syringe is halved, the molecules are confined to a smaller space. Even though they are moving at the same average speed, they now have a shorter distance to travel between the walls of the container. 

Because the travel distance is reduced, the gas molecules will strike the internal walls of the syringe more often. Therefore, the **frequency of collisions** with the walls increases.

highlights.text: the molecules hit the walls more frequently

steps.title: Determine pressure change
steps.content:
Pressure is defined as the average force exerted by these molecular collisions per unit area of the wall. 

Since the molecules are hitting the walls more frequently (more collisions per second), the total force exerted on the walls increases. As a result, the pressure of the air inside the syringe increases.

highlights.text: this results in an increase in pressure

steps.title: Link Temperature and Speed
steps.content:
In kinetic theory, the temperature of a gas is a measure of the average random kinetic energy of its molecules. The question states that the temperature is kept constant.

From the data booklet, we see the relationship:
$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

Since $T$ is constant, the average kinetic energy $(\overline{E_k})$ and therefore the average speed of the molecules remains unchanged.

steps.title: Consider Momentum per Collision
steps.content:
When a gas molecule strikes the wall of the syringe and bounces back, its momentum changes. Since momentum is $p = mv$, the change in momentum $(\Delta p)$ for an individual collision depends on the mass and the velocity of the molecule.

Because the average speed of the molecules is constant (due to constant temperature), the **change in momentum per individual collision** remains the same.

steps.title: Analyze Collision Frequency
steps.content:
Now, we look at the effect of volume. The question says the volume is halved. 

With the molecules packed into a smaller space, they have a shorter distance to travel before hitting a wall. This results in the molecules hitting the walls **more frequently**.

highlights.text: volume of the air is halved

steps.title: Relate Force to Pressure
steps.content:
Pressure is defined as the force per unit area $(P = \frac{F}{A})$. According to Newton's Second Law, force is the rate of change of momentum:

$$F = \frac{\Delta p_{total}}{\Delta t}$$

Even though the momentum change per collision is constant, the **increased frequency of collisions** means there is a higher total change in momentum per second. This leads to a greater average force on the walls and, consequently, an **increase in pressure**.

steps.title: Analyze the given conditions
steps.content:
To understand how pressure changes, we first look at the state of the gas. The question specifies that we have a fixed mass of air and that the **volume is halved** while **keeping the temperature constant**.

From **Topic B.3 (Gas laws)**, we know that temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the molecules. According to the formula:
$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$
Since the temperature $T$ remains constant, the average speed and kinetic energy of the molecules do not change.

highlights.text: volume of the air is halved

steps.title: Evaluate molecular number density
steps.content:
Using the **Molecular Density Approach**, we focus on how many molecules are in a specific space. 

Because the mass is fixed, the total number of molecules ($N$) remains the same. However, these molecules are now packed into half the original volume ($V$). This means the **number density** (the number of molecules per unit volume, $\frac{N}{V}$) has doubled.

steps.title: Relate density to collisions
steps.content:
Because the molecules are more crowded (higher number density), there are more molecules available to strike the walls of the container at any given moment. 

Even though the molecules are moving at the same average speed as before, the reduced space means they have shorter distances to travel between the walls. Consequently, the **molecules hit the walls more frequently** (more impacts per second per unit area).

steps.title: Conclude the pressure change
steps.content:
Pressure is defined as the force exerted by these molecular collisions per unit area of the wall. 

Since the frequency of collisions has increased while the average force of each individual impact (determined by the constant temperature) remains the same, the total force per unit area increases. Therefore, the **pressure of the gas increases**.

In this specific case, because the volume was halved at a constant temperature, the pressure will double (following Boyle's Law, $PV = \text{constant}$).

parts.content: State the law that relates pressure and volume under these conditions.
parts.markscheme:
Boyle’s Law (pressure is inversely proportional to volume at constant temperature) <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 1 mark]

parts.content:
Initially, the air in the syringe is at a pressure of $101 \text{ kPa}$, temperature of $300 \text{ K}$, and volume of $60.0 \text{ cm}^3$.

Calculate the number of moles of air in the syringe. Assume ideal gas behavior.
parts.markscheme:
Perform the unit conversion for volume ($60.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$) <marks>M1</marks>

Substitute values into the ideal gas law $n = \frac{pV}{RT}$: 
$$n = \frac{101000 \times 60.0 \times 10^{-6}}{8.31 \times 300}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

Calculate the final answer: $n = 0.00243 \text{ mol}$ <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 3 marks]

steps.title: Identify the ideal gas law
steps.content:
To find the number of moles of air, we use the **Ideal Gas Law** equation found in the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet. This equation relates pressure ($P$), volume ($V$), and temperature ($T$) for an ideal gas.

$$PV = nRT$$

steps.title: Convert units to SI
steps.content:
Before substituting values, we must ensure all quantities are in standard SI units to match the gas constant $R = 8.31 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$:

*   **Pressure ($P$):** $101 \text{ kPa} = 101 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$
*   **Volume ($V$):** $60.0 \text{ cm}^3 = 60.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
*   **Temperature ($T$):** $300 \text{ K}$ (already in Kelvin)

Converting the volume correctly to cubic meters is worth the first mark in the markscheme.

steps.title: Rearrange and substitute
steps.content:
Rearrange the equation to solve for the number of moles ($n$):

$$n = \frac{PV}{RT}$$

Now, substitute our converted values into the formula:

$$n = \frac{(101 \times 10^3) \times (60.0 \times 10^{-6})}{8.31 \times 300}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Using your calculator, solve for $n$ to three significant figures, as given in the question data.

$$n = 0.00243 \text{ mol}$$

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into the input bar. Use 'e' for scientific notation or 10^n.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles using the ideal gas equation.

steps.title: Convert units to SI
steps.content:
To use the ideal gas law equations, we must first ensure all values are in SI units:

*   **Pressure ($P$):** $101 \text{ kPa} = 101 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$ or $101,000 \text{ Pa}$
*   **Volume ($V$):** $60.0 \text{ cm}^3 = 60.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
*   **Temperature ($T$):** $300 \text{ K}$ (already in Kelvin)

Converting the volume to cubic meters is a critical step for earning the first mark.

steps.title: Find total molecules (N)
steps.content:
Using the version of the ideal gas law that includes the Boltzmann constant ($k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J K}^{-1}$), we calculate the total number of molecules ($N$):

$$PV = Nk_BT$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$N = \frac{PV}{k_BT}$$

Substituting the values:

$$N = \frac{101,000 \times 60.0 \times 10^{-6}}{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}$$

$$N \approx 1.4638 \times 10^{21} \text{ molecules}$$

gdc.instructions: Use the fraction template for clarity.

ti84.instructions: Enter the numerator in parentheses, then divide by the denominator in parentheses.

desmos.instructions: Type the equation directly into the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the total number of molecules N.

steps.title: Convert to moles (n)
steps.content:
Finally, we convert the total number of molecules into moles ($n$) using the Avogadro constant ($N_A = 6.02 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$):

$$n = \frac{N}{N_A}$$

Substituting our value for $N$:

$$n = \frac{1.4638 \times 10^{21}}{6.02 \times 10^{23}}$$

$$n \approx 0.00243 \text{ mol}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the previous result by 6.02 x 10^23.

ti84.instructions: Divide the previous answer (Ans) by Avogadro's constant.

desmos.instructions: Divide the result from the previous step by Avogadro's constant.

calculator.description: Calculate the number of moles n.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The number of moles of air in the syringe is $0.00243 \text{ mol}$. 

Marks are awarded for:
1.  Correct volume unit conversion ($60.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$).
2.  Correct substitution into the gas law relationship.
3.  Final numerical answer with correct precision.

parts.content:
The syringe is now placed in a water bath at $360 \text{ K}$, and the plunger is allowed to move freely.

Describe what happens to the volume of the gas.
parts.markscheme:
Describe that increasing temperature increases the average kinetic energy (and speed) of the gas molecules <marks>R1</marks>

State that the pressure remains constant because the plunger is free to move <marks>R1</marks>

Conclude that the volume must increase <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 3 marks]

steps.title: Relate temperature to kinetic energy
steps.content:
When the syringe is placed in the $360 \text{ K}$ water bath, the temperature of the gas increases. According to the kinetic theory of gases, the absolute temperature $T$ is directly proportional to the average kinetic energy of the molecules. 

We can see this relationship in the data booklet:
$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

As $T$ increases, the **average kinetic energy** (and therefore the **average speed**) of the gas molecules increases.

steps.title: Analyze pressure conditions
steps.content: The question states that the plunger can **move freely** and without friction. This is a crucial detail because it means the plunger will always adjust its position until the internal pressure of the gas equals the external atmospheric pressure. Since the external pressure remains constant, the **internal pressure of the gas must also remain constant**.

highlights.text: plunger is allowed to move freely

steps.title: Determine the volume change
steps.content:
Pressure is defined as the force per unit area exerted by molecules colliding with the walls. 

Because the molecules are now moving faster (due to higher $T$), they hit the plunger more forcefully and more often. To keep the pressure constant, the **volume must increase**. 

By increasing the volume, the molecules have to travel further between collisions, which reduces the frequency of collisions with the plunger. This reduction in frequency perfectly offsets the increased force of each collision, maintaining a constant pressure. Therefore, the **volume increases**.

steps.title: Analyze Temperature and Energy
steps.content:
When the syringe is placed in a water bath at $360 \text{ K}$, the temperature of the air inside increases (since room temperature is approximately $293 \text{ K}$). According to the kinetic theory of gases, the absolute temperature is directly proportional to the average kinetic energy of the molecules.

$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

Therefore, increasing the temperature increases the average kinetic energy (and speed) of the gas molecules.

steps.title: Identify Constant Pressure
steps.content: The question states that the plunger can move without friction and is allowed to move freely. This means that as the gas is heated, the plunger will adjust its position until the internal pressure of the gas equals the external atmospheric pressure. Since the external pressure is constant, the pressure $P$ of the gas remains constant.

highlights.text: plunger is allowed to move freely

steps.title: Apply the Ideal Gas Law
steps.content:
We can use the Ideal Gas Law to determine the effect on volume:

$$PV=nRT$$

Because the syringe is sealed, the number of moles ($n$) is constant. Since the pressure ($P$) is also constant and $R$ is a constant, the volume is directly proportional to the temperature ($V \propto T$). This relationship is known as Charles's Law.

steps.title: Conclude the Volume Change
steps.content: Since the temperature $T$ increases from room temperature to $360 \text{ K}$, and the relationship is $V \propto T$, the volume $V$ of the gas must increase.

parts.content: Calculate the new volume of the gas.
parts.markscheme:
State the relationship $\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$ or $V \propto T$ <marks>M1</marks>

Substitute the known values:
$$\frac{60.0}{300} = \frac{V_2}{360}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

Calculate the final volume:
$$V_2 = \frac{60.0 \times 360}{300} = 72.0 \text{ cm}^3$$ <marks>A1</marks>

[Total: 3 marks]

questions.specification:
A sealed vertical cylinder contains a fixed mass of ideal gas. Initially, the gas occupies volume $V_0$ and is at pressure $P_0$ and temperature $T_0$.

The gas is compressed rapidly to volume $\dfrac{1}{2}V_0$. During the process, the temperature of the gas increases to $T_1$.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/OvjPqvOQHGl1qaogzOdP8.jpeg)

Assume the gas behaves ideally throughout, and the number of moles $n$ remains constant.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: B.3.1 Kinetic theory and gas behavior

parts.content: Deduce an expression for the final pressure $P_1$ in terms of $P_0$, $T_1$, and $T_0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Use $pV = nRT$ for both initial and final states: $\frac{P_0V_0}{T_0}=\frac{P_1(\frac{1}{2}V_0)}{T_1}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Express the final pressure: $P_1=2P_0 \frac{T_1}{T_0}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Recall the Ideal Gas Law
steps.content:
To solve this, we start with the ideal gas law from the data booklet. Since the question states we have a fixed mass of gas (the number of moles $n$ is constant) and $R$ is a constant, we can rearrange the equation to see that the ratio of $PV$ to $T$ remains constant throughout the process.

$$\frac{PV}{T} = nR = \text{constant}$$

steps.title: Apply the Combined Gas Law
steps.content:
Because the gas is in a sealed cylinder, the initial and final states must satisfy the combined gas law:

$$\frac{P_0 V_0}{T_0} = \frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1}$$

This equation allows us to compare the pressure, volume, and temperature before and after the compression.

steps.title: Substitute the known values
steps.content:
The question specifies that the gas is compressed to a final volume of $\frac{1}{2}V_0$. Let's substitute $V_1 = \frac{1}{2}V_0$ into our equation:

$$\frac{P_0 V_0}{T_0} = \frac{P_1 (\frac{1}{2}V_0)}{T_1}$$

steps.title: Solve for final pressure
steps.content:
Now we rearrange the equation to isolate $P_1$. First, we can cancel $V_0$ from both sides, as it is non-zero:

$$\frac{P_0}{T_0} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} P_1}{T_1}$$

Next, multiply both sides by $T_1$ and then by $2$ to solve for $P_1$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{P_0 T_1}{T_0} &= \frac{1}{2} P_1 \\ P_1 &= 2 P_0 \frac{T_1}{T_0} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the requirement to express the final pressure in terms of the given variables.

steps.title: Identify the initial state
steps.content:
First, we identify the variables for the gas in its initial state. The question tells us the gas is initially at pressure $P_0$, volume $V_0$, and temperature $T_0$. We can relate these using the ideal gas law from the **Thermal Physics** section of your data booklet.

For the initial state:
$$P_0 V_0 = nRT_0$$

highlights.text: initially, the gas occupies volume V0 and is at pressure P0 and temperature T0

steps.title: Identify the final state
steps.content:
Next, we look at the final state after the compression. The gas is compressed to a new volume, and the temperature increases. 

Given information for the final state:
- Final Pressure $= P_1$
- Final Volume $= \frac{1}{2}V_0$
- Final Temperature $= T_1$

Applying the ideal gas law again:
$$P_1 \left( \frac{1}{2}V_0 \right) = nRT_1$$

highlights.text: temperature of the gas increases to T1

steps.title: Apply the ratio method
steps.content:
To find $P_1$, we can divide the equation for the final state by the equation for the initial state. Since the mass is fixed, the number of moles $n$ and the gas constant $R$ remain constant and will cancel out.

$$\frac{P_1 \left( \frac{1}{2}V_0 \right)}{P_0 V_0} = \frac{nRT_1}{nRT_0}$$

steps.title: Simplify and solve
steps.content:
Now we simplify the expression. The volume $V_0$ cancels out on the left side, and $nR$ cancels out on the right side:

$$\frac{\frac{1}{2}P_1}{P_0} = \frac{T_1}{T_0}$$

To isolate $P_1$, multiply both sides by $2P_0$:

$$P_1 = 2P_0 \left( \frac{T_1}{T_0} \right)$$

This final expression shows that the pressure increases due to both the decrease in volume (the factor of 2) and the change in temperature.

gdc.instructions: Divide 1 by 0.5 to verify the multiplier for pressure.

ti84.instructions: Divide 1 by 0.5 to see how the pressure is affected by the volume change alone.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the reciprocal of the volume change factor (1/2).

calculator.description: Verifying the numerical coefficient when volume is halved.

steps.title: Identify the proportionality relationship
steps.content:
To solve this using proportionality, we look at the ideal gas law from the data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

Since we have a "fixed mass" (meaning $n$ is constant) and $R$ is the gas constant, we can see that pressure $P$ is directly proportional to temperature $T$ and inversely proportional to volume $V$. This can be written as:

$$P \propto \frac{T}{V}$$

steps.title: Analyze the volume change
steps.content:
The gas is compressed to half its initial volume, so the final volume is $\frac{1}{2}V_0$. 

Because pressure is inversely proportional to volume ($P \propto \frac{1}{V}$), halving the volume causes the pressure to increase by a factor of 2. 

If temperature had stayed constant, the pressure would have become $2P_0$.

steps.title: Analyze the temperature change
steps.content:
Next, we consider the change in temperature from $T_0$ to $T_1$. 

Since pressure is directly proportional to temperature ($P \propto T$), the pressure is further modified by the ratio of the final temperature to the initial temperature:

$$\text{Temperature factor} = \frac{T_1}{T_0}$$

highlights.text: temperature of the gas increases to T_1

steps.title: Combine factors for final pressure
steps.content:
Now we combine both effects to find the final pressure $P_1$. We take the initial pressure $P_0$ and multiply it by the volume factor (2) and the temperature factor ($\frac{T_1}{T_0}$):

$$P_1 = P_0 \times 2 \times \frac{T_1}{T_0}$$

Which simplifies to:

$$P_1 = 2P_0 \frac{T_1}{T_0}$$

parts.content:
The final temperature is $T_1 = 1.5T_0$.
Determine the final pressure $P_1$ in terms of $P_0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $T_1 = 1.5T_0$ into the expression: $P_1=2P_0 \frac{1.5T_0}{T_0}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final pressure: $P_1=3P_0$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify known gas states
steps.content:
First, let's list the initial and final states of the gas provided in the problem. This helps us see clearly what changed.

**Initial State:**
- Pressure: $P_0$
- Volume: $V_0$
- Temperature: $T_0$

**Final State:**
- Pressure: $P_1$ (what we want to find)
- Volume: $V_1 = \frac{1}{2}V_0$
- Temperature: $T_1 = 1.5T_0$

steps.title: Apply the Combined Gas Law
steps.content:
Since we have a **fixed mass of ideal gas**, the number of moles ($n$) is constant. From the ideal gas law, we know that the ratio $\frac{PV}{T}$ is constant. This is known as the Combined Gas Law:

$$\frac{P_0 V_0}{T_0} = \frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1}$$

This relationship allows us to compare two different states of the same gas sample.

steps.title: Substitute and rearrange
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the final conditions into our equation and rearrange to solve for $P_1$:

$$\frac{P_0 V_0}{T_0} = \frac{P_1 (0.5 V_0)}{1.5 T_0}$$

We can cancel out $V_0$ and $T_0$ from both sides, as they are non-zero constants:

$$\frac{P_0}{1} = \frac{P_1 \times 0.5}{1.5}$$

Multiplying both sides by $1.5$ and dividing by $0.5$ gives us:

$$P_1 = P_0 \times \frac{1.5}{0.5}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final pressure
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the numerical ratio to find $P_1$ in terms of $P_0$.

$$P_1 = 3P_0$$

This makes sense physically: compressing the gas to half its volume would double the pressure ($2P_0$) if temperature were constant. Since the temperature also increased by 50%, the pressure increases further to $3P_0$.

ti84.instructions: Enter the division in the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression into a new cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the temperature change over the volume change factor.

steps.title: Identify the proportionality
steps.content:
To solve this using proportionality reasoning, we first look at the **Ideal Gas Law** from the data booklet. This topic is covered in **Topic B.3: Gas laws**. Since the number of moles $n$ and the gas constant $R$ are fixed, we can see how pressure relates to volume and temperature:

$$PV = nRT \implies P \propto \frac{T}{V}$$

This tells us that the pressure is **directly proportional** to the absolute temperature and **inversely proportional** to the volume.

steps.title: Effect of volume change
steps.content:
First, let's look at the change in volume. The gas is compressed from $V_0$ to $\frac{1}{2}V_0$.

Because $P \propto \frac{1}{V}$, halving the volume causes the pressure to **double**. 

$$\text{Pressure factor from volume} = 2$$

steps.title: Effect of temperature change
steps.content:
Next, we consider the temperature change. The temperature increases from $T_0$ to $1.5T_0$.

Because $P \propto T$, increasing the temperature by a factor of $1.5$ increases the pressure by that same factor.

$$\text{Pressure factor from temperature} = 1.5$$

steps.title: Calculate final pressure
steps.content:
To find the final pressure $P_1$, we multiply the initial pressure $P_0$ by both factors we identified:

$$\begin{aligned} P_1 &= P_0 \times 2 \times 1.5 \\ P_1 &= P_0 \times 3 \end{aligned}$$

This matches the markscheme requirement where we substitute the change in temperature into the relationship derived from the volume change.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the product of the two proportionality factors.

ti84.instructions: Enter the volume factor multiplied by the temperature factor.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the product of the multipliers.

calculator.description: Calculate the total multiplier for the pressure.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Therefore, the final pressure is:

$$P_1 = 3P_0$$

parts.content: Explain, using molecular theory, why compressing the gas rapidly causes an increase in temperature.
parts.markscheme:
- State that during rapid compression, gas molecules are forced into a smaller volume and collide more frequently with the moving piston and walls <marks>R1</marks>
- Explain that this increases the average kinetic energy of the molecules, which corresponds to a rise in temperature <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the piston's motion
steps.content: When the gas is compressed, the piston moves inward (downward in this case) toward the gas molecules. To understand why the temperature increases, we need to look at what happens at the molecular level when a gas particle hits this moving surface. This relates to **Topic B.3: Gas laws**, specifically the microscopic basis for macroscopic behavior.

steps.title: Collision with the piston
steps.content:
As the gas molecules collide with the inward-moving piston, they behave like a ball hitting a moving bat. Because the piston is moving towards the molecules, the molecules rebound with a **higher speed** than they had before the collision. 

This gain in speed means that each molecule's individual kinetic energy ($E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$) increases.

steps.title: Linking energy to temperature
steps.content:
The temperature of an ideal gas is a direct measure of the average random kinetic energy of its molecules. We can see this relationship in the Thermal Physics section of the data booklet:

$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

Since the collisions with the moving piston increase the average kinetic energy ($\overline{E_{k}}$) of the gas molecules, the absolute temperature ($T$) must also increase. The 'rapid' nature of the compression ensures there is little time for this energy to escape as heat, leading to the observed rise in temperature.

steps.title: Analyze the piston interaction
steps.content: When the gas is **compressed rapidly**, the piston moves inward toward the gas molecules. As the molecules collide with the moving piston, they rebound with a higher speed than they had before the collision. This is because the piston does work on the individual gas molecules during the interaction.

steps.title: Link work to energy
steps.content: Since the process is rapid, there is no time for thermal energy to leave the cylinder. The work done on the gas by the piston is transferred directly into the **internal energy** of the gas. On a molecular level, the increased speed of the rebounding molecules means their average kinetic energy increases.

steps.title: Relate kinetic energy to temperature
steps.content:
According to the kinetic theory of gases, the absolute temperature $T$ of an ideal gas is directly proportional to the average random kinetic energy $\overline{E_{k}}$ of its molecules. We can see this relationship in the Thermal Physics section of the data booklet:

$$\overline{E_{k}} = \frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

Since the average kinetic energy has increased due to the work done by the piston, the temperature of the gas must also increase.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure you clearly state:
1. The moving piston does work on the molecules (increasing their speed/kinetic energy during collisions).
2. The increase in **average kinetic energy** directly results in an increase in **absolute temperature**.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. This value might be obtained by incorrectly assuming the number of molecules is inversely proportional to the product of pressure and temperature ($N \propto \frac{1}{PT}$).

Correct. According to the ideal gas law in the form $PV = N k_B T$, the number of molecules $N$ is given by $N = \frac{PV}{k_B T}$. For container 1, $N_1 = \frac{PV}{k_B T}$. For container 2, the volume $V$ is the same, so $N_2 = \frac{(4P)V}{k_B (2T)} = 2\frac{PV}{k_B T}$. The ratio $\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.50$.
Incorrect. This is the value of the ratio $\frac{N_2}{N_1}$ rather than $\frac{N_1}{N_2}$.

methods.label: Direct use of Ideal Gas Equation

steps.title: Identify the final conditions
steps.content:
To find the new volume, we first need to list the values for the gas in its final state. The question tells us that the number of moles and the pressure remain the same, while the temperature increases.

- Number of moles ($n$): $2\text{ mol}$
- Final temperature ($T$): $600\text{ K}$
- Pressure ($P$): $100\text{ kPa} = 100 \times 10^3\text{ Pa}$
- Gas constant ($R$): $8.31\text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$

highlights.text: keeping the number of moles and pressure constant

steps.title: Select the Ideal Gas Equation
steps.content: In **Topic B.3 (Gas Laws)**, we learn that the behavior of an ideal gas is described by the ideal gas equation found in your data booklet. Since we are looking for volume ($V$) and have $P$, $n$, and $T$, this is the most direct formula to use.

steps.title: Calculate the new volume
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the equation to solve for $V$ and substitute our values. 

$$\begin{aligned} V &= \frac{nRT}{P} \\ V &= \frac{2 \times 8.31 \times 600}{100 \times 10^3} \\ V &= 0.09972 \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned}$$

To provide the answer in the commonly used unit of $\text{dm}^3$, we multiply by $1000$:
$$V \approx 99.7 \text{ dm}^3$$

gdc.instructions: Enter (2 * 8.31 * 600) / (100 * 10^3) into the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression (2 * 8.31 * 600) / (100 * 10^3) and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the formula directly into a cell: V = (2 * 8.31 * 600) / (100 * 10^3)

calculator.description: Calculate the final volume using the ideal gas law.

steps.title: Identify the Constant Conditions
steps.content: In this problem, we are told that the **number of moles** and the **pressure** remain constant while the temperature changes. This situation is described by **Topic B.3 Gas Laws** in your syllabus. Specifically, when pressure and moles are fixed, the volume of an ideal gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature ($V \propto T$).

highlights.text: number of moles and pressure constant

steps.title: State Charles's Law Relationship
steps.content:
Since volume is proportional to temperature, we can use the ratio known as Charles's Law:

$$\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$$

Where:
- $V_1$ and $T_1$ are the initial volume and temperature.
- $V_2$ and $T_2$ are the new volume and temperature.

steps.title: Establish Initial Volume
steps.content:
Using the ideal gas equation from the data booklet:

$$PV = nRT$$

We first find the initial volume ($V_1$) using the values provided in the question: $n = 2$, $R = 8.31$, $T_1 = 300\text{ K}$, and $P = 100 \times 10^3\text{ Pa}$.

$$V_1 = \frac{nRT_1}{P} = \frac{2 \times 8.31 \times 300}{100 \times 10^3} = 0.04986\text{ m}^3$$

steps.title: Calculate the New Volume
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the Charles's Law formula to solve for $V_2$:

$$V_2 = V_1 \times \frac{T_2}{T_1}$$

Substituting our values ($T_1 = 300\text{ K}$ and $T_2 = 600\text{ K}$):

$$V_2 = 0.04986 \times \frac{600}{300}$$

Since the temperature exactly doubled, the volume must also double.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the product of initial volume and temperature ratio.

ti84.instructions: Enter the initial volume multiplied by the ratio of temperatures.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the initial volume by the ratio of final to initial temperature.

calculator.description: Calculate the new volume by doubling the initial volume.

steps.title: State Final Result
steps.content:
The new volume is $0.09972\text{ m}^3$. Rounding to three significant figures as is standard in IB Physics:

$$V_2 = 0.0997\text{ m}^3$$

Alternatively, you can express this in decimetres cubed ($1\text{ m}^3 = 1000\text{ dm}^3$):

$$V_2 = 99.7\text{ dm}^3$$
